docs.title: Blocker
docs.text:
T02: Seniority variant direction disagreement. A native_backfill bug in Stage 5 (resolve_seniority_final(), line 624) causes seniority_final to ignore 9,490 SWE rows with usable seniority_native labels. Only 1 of 30,283 SWE rows gets backfilled. As a result: - Rule-based variants (seniority_final, high-confidence) show entry share increasing (0.8% -> 4.4% -> 5.7%) - Native-label variants show entry share decreasing (22.9% -> 15.5%) - The RQ1 conclusion flips depending on which operationalization is used.

Decision required: Fix the bug and re-run preprocessing before Wave 2, or proceed with Wave 2 noting the constraint?

docs.title: Warnings
docs.text:
1. Seniority unknown rate 29-55% for SWE — all seniority-stratified analyses use <71% of data (T01/T03)
  2. Entry-level samples thin — 89 (arshkon) / 132 (scraped) for seniority_final (T03)
  3. Asaniczka "associate" overlaps with arshkon "entry" — 60 shared titles; cross-source seniority unreliable without re-labeling (T03)
  4. embedding_llm tier 26% FP rate — 14% of SWE rows; recommend regex-only sensitivity check (T04)
  5. Description length grew 50% — keyword counts must be normalized by length (T05)
  6. Industry labels incomparable — scraped compound labels vs arshkon single labels (T05)
  7. Asaniczka company concentration — Capital One + Recruiting from Scratch = 15.3% of SWE postings (T06)
  8. "Jobs via Dice" possible aggregator leakage — 130 scraped postings (3.1%) (T06)

docs.title: Clean
docs.text:
• Geographic representativeness exceeds target: all r > 0.93, combined r = 0.978 (T07)
  • All collection periods in same labor market regime: Indeed SWE index 69.9-71.9 (T07)
  • Company-capped sensitivity robust: seniority shifts <1.5pp after capping at 10/company (T06)
  • SWE classification adequate: overall FP ~5.5%, below 10% threshold (T04)

Seniority column recommendation: Use seniority_patched from the SQL workaround in exploration/seniority_workaround.sql. This replicates what the fixed pipeline will produce by applying: strong title signals > native backfill > weak signals > unknown. Results:

Source seniority_final unknown % seniority_patched unknown % Old entry n New entry n arshkon 54.6% 19.9% 89 745 asaniczka 29.3% 0.0% 124 121 scraped 44.6% 6.4% 132 549

Wave 2 agents: include the swe_patched CTE from exploration/seniority_workaround.sql in all seniority-stratified queries. Use seniority_patched instead of seniority_final, and seniority_3level_patched instead of seniority_3level. Also report results using the original seniority_final as a sensitivity variant.

Column exclusions for cross-period analysis: company_industry (asaniczka=0%), company_size (arshkon-only), skills_raw (asaniczka-only), work_type (asaniczka=0%). All requirement/skill analysis must use description-text NLP.

docs.title: Gate 2 checklist
docs.text:
• [x] T08: Distributions show expected patterns. Entry share 21.0%->13.9% (patched). Description length +64%. YOE coverage dropped to 16% in 2026 (warning).
  • [x] T09: 3 of 5 variants agree on entry decline (-7pp). 2 title-only variants disagree (+1.5pp). Native-label cluster is better-powered (545-745 vs 87-132). Not a new blocker.
  • [x] T10: AI is the dominant distinguisher. Ownership/autonomy language shifted to 2026. Findings strongly align with RQ1/RQ2.
  • [x] T11: Entry JSD 52% higher than overall — entry-level postings changed disproportionately. YOE decreased (3.7->2.0). AI prevalence 19%->55%.
  • [x] T12: Scope inflation confirmed for organizational language but NOT formal credentials. Field-wide, not junior-specific.
  • [x] T13: Within-company entry decline -9.3pp (stronger than overall -3.8pp). AI surge is within-company, not compositional. Large firms drive the squeeze.
  • [x] T14: Employer-worker AI gap closed from ~40pp to ~0pp (2024->2026). AI-tool category (3%->26%) is cleanest RQ3 variable.
  • [x] T15: No systematic data quality issues. Ghost risk flag too conservative for use; needs LLM assessment.
  • [x] T16: Strongest finding — SWE junior decline is occupation-specific. Control group shows opposite trend (+21pp). Rules out macro confounding.

docs.title: Warnings
docs.text:
1. T09: Seniority direction not fully resolved — 3/5 variants agree on decline, but seniority_llm still needed for definitive answer
  2. T08: YOE extraction coverage dropped from 34-40% to 16.4% in 2026 — YOE findings are directionally useful but selection-biased
  3. T12: Scope inflation is field-wide — not disproportionately junior; complicates the "junior squeeze" narrative
  4. T13: Aggregator contamination in overlap set — TEKsystems, Apex Systems show erratic seniority labels; sensitivity check needed excluding is_aggregator
  5. T12: Collaboration keyword inflation may partly reflect longer descriptions having more boilerplate

docs.title: Findings to reconcile in synthesis
docs.text:
• YOE paradox: Entry-level YOE decreased (T11, T12) while organizational scope increased. Not credential inflation but complexity inflation.
  • Field-wide vs junior-specific: Scope inflation exists at all levels (T12), but entry decline is SWE-specific (T16) and within-company (T13).
  • Within-company vs composition: Entry decline -9.3pp within-company, partially masked by +5.6pp composition effect from new companies (T13).
  • AI convergence: Employer AI requirements caught up with worker usage (T14), consistent with rapid institutionalization narrative.

docs.title: Exploration Synthesis
docs.text: Date: 2026-03-23 Coverage: T01-T17 (16 data audit, validation, and analysis tasks + 1 artifact task) Purpose: Single handoff document for the analysis agent. Read this first.

docs.title: RQ1: Employer-Side Restructuring (Junior Share, Scope Inflation, Senior Redefinition)
docs.text:
Can we answer it? Yes, with caveats. The headline finding is robust: SWE entry-level share declined from 21.0% to 13.9% between April 2024 and March 2026 (seniority_patched). This finding survives: - Within-company decomposition: -9.3pp within 379 overlapping companies (T13) - Cross-occupation test: Control group shows opposite trend (+21pp), ruling out macro confounding (T16) - Company-capping sensitivity: <1.5pp shift after capping at 10 postings/company (T06) - 3 of 5 seniority variants agree on decline direction (T09)

Constraints: - Direction depends on seniority operationalization: 3 native-label variants show decline, 2 title-only variants show increase (T09). The native-label cluster has 5-8x more entry observations and is the recommended primary. - Historical entry-level baseline rests entirely on arshkon (n=745 entry after patching). Asaniczka contributes zero entry-level labels (T03). - seniority_llm has not run; the patched workaround is the best available but is not the definitive classification. - Scope inflation is field-wide, not junior-specific (T12). Organizational language (cross-functional +10pp, ownership +8pp) increased at all seniority levels. But formal credential requirements (YOE, MS/PhD) did NOT inflate -- entry-level YOE actually decreased from 3.0 to 1.0 years (T11, T12).

docs.title: RQ2: Task and Requirement Migration
docs.text:
Can we answer it? Yes. Text-based analyses are well-powered and the key findings are strong: - AI/ML terminology is the dominant temporal distinguisher across all comparisons (T10 Fightin' Words) - AI prevalence surged 19% -> 55% (broad) and 3.6% -> 24% (narrow LLM-specific) (T11) - Ownership/autonomy language shifted toward 2026 while traditional "management" language favors 2024 (T10) - 74 newly emerging terms are dominated by AI tooling: agentic, claude, gemini, embeddings, mcp, cursor (T11) - Entry-level JSD (0.059) is 52% higher than overall JSD (0.038), confirming entry-level postings changed disproportionately (T11)

Constraints: - Description length grew 64% (median 3124 -> 5110). All keyword counts must be normalized by length; binary indicators are less affected but not immune (T05, T08). - Boilerplate removal accuracy is ~44%. Use description (not description_core) for keyword/pattern work (T01). - Skills fields (skills_raw) are not available cross-period. All requirement analysis must use description-text NLP (T03).

docs.title: RQ3: Employer-Requirement / Worker-Usage Divergence
docs.text:
Can we answer it? Yes. T14 provides clean results: - Employer AI requirements tripled: 18% -> 55% (any AI), 3% -> 26% (AI tools) (T14) - Worker-side benchmark (StackOverflow): 62% active use in 2024, 51% daily use in 2025 - Gap closed from ~40pp to ~0pp between 2024 and 2026 (T14) - AI-tool-specific category (LLM/GPT/Copilot) is the cleanest variable: 3% -> 26% (T14)

Constraints: - Worker-side data is survey-based (self-reported) and measures different constructs than posting keywords (T14). - Cannot distinguish whether convergence is driven by same companies updating requirements vs. AI-heavy companies posting more in 2026 (compositional effect). T13's within-company analysis shows AI surge is +33pp within-company vs. +1.5pp composition, supporting genuine updating.

docs.title: RQ4: Mechanisms (Interview-Based)
docs.text: Can we answer it? The quantitative foundation is strong. Five interview elicitation artifacts are ready (T17): 1. Scope-inflated entry-level JDs 2. Same-company paired JDs (2024 vs 2026) 3. Junior-share decline + AI model release timeline 4. Senior archetype shift chart 5. Employer-worker AI divergence chart

docs.title: 3. Seniority Column Recommendation
docs.text: Use seniority_patched from exploration/seniority_workaround.sql as the primary analysis variable.

docs.title: Why
docs.text:
The pipeline's seniority_final has a native_backfill bug (T02) that causes it to ignore 9,490 SWE rows with usable seniority_native labels. The seniority_patched workaround applies the correct priority order:

  1. Title-strong signals (title_keyword, title_manager) -- highest priority
  2. Native backfill (seniority_native) -- second priority
  3. Weak title signals -- third priority
  4. Unknown -- fallback

docs.text:
Always report results using both seniority_patched (primary) and seniority_final (sensitivity). The variant table from T09 shows:

docs.title: Pending fix
docs.text: Stage 5 infer_seniority_family() needs to align with is_swe classification (T02). After the fix, re-run preprocessing and use seniority_final directly. Also run seniority_llm (Stage 10/11) for an independent third signal.

docs.title: 4.1 Description length growth
docs.text:
• Median description length grew 64% (3,124 -> 5,110 chars) from 2024 to 2026 (T05, T08)
  • Mitigation: Normalize keyword counts by description_length. Use binary indicators (any mention) as primary; rate-per-1K-chars as sensitivity. T12 showed length-normalized tech density barely changed (1.2 -> 1.3 per 1K chars) while raw counts increased.

docs.title: 4.2 Asaniczka label gap
docs.text:
• asaniczka has zero entry-level native labels (T03). Its seniority distribution is 100% mid-senior + associate.
  • Mitigation: Exclude asaniczka from all entry-level trend analyses. Use only arshkon (2024-04) vs scraped (2026-03) for seniority-stratified comparisons. Asaniczka is useful only for mid-senior subgroup analyses and as volume context.

docs.title: 4.3 Aggregator contamination
docs.text:
• Aggregator rates differ by source: asaniczka 28%, arshkon 12%, scraped 14.5% (T08)
  • "Jobs via Dice" (130 scraped rows) likely an undetected aggregator (T06, T13)
  • TEKsystems, Apex Systems show erratic seniority labels in overlap analysis (T13)
  • Mitigation: Run all key analyses with is_aggregator = false as sensitivity check. Flag "Jobs via Dice" and "Turing" for exclusion.

docs.title: 4.4 Field-wide scope inflation
docs.text:
• Organizational language (cross-functional, ownership, end-to-end, collaboration) increased at ALL seniority levels, not just entry (T12)
  • Mitigation: Report the field-wide baseline shift alongside entry-level-specific findings. The claim is not "employers are piling requirements onto juniors uniquely" but rather "the entire SWE profession is experiencing scope inflation, and entry-level roles are not exempt despite their lower formal requirements (YOE declined)."

docs.title: 4.5 Company concentration (asaniczka)
docs.text:
• Capital One + Recruiting from Scratch = 15.3% of asaniczka SWE postings (T06)
  • Mitigation: Company-capped sensitivity (<1.5pp shift after capping at 10/company). Also report asaniczka results with and without top-2 companies.

docs.title: 4.6 SWE classification noise
docs.text:
• Overall FP rate ~5.5%; embedding_llm tier has 26% FP rate (T04)
  • Mitigation: Regex-tier-only sensitivity (84% of SWE rows, ~2% FP). Report main results on full sample, sensitivity on regex-only.

docs.title: 6.1 YOE decrease vs. scope increase
docs.text:
Entry-level YOE requirements dropped (mean 3.7 -> 2.0 years, T11) while organizational scope increased (cross-functional +10pp, ownership +8pp, T12). This is not credential inflation but complexity inflation: employers want fewer formal years but more organizational maturity. Possible mechanisms: - AI tools reduce the experience needed for technical tasks, shifting emphasis to soft/organizational skills - The definition of "entry-level" is expanding to include more organizational scope as a substitute for technical experience - The YOE decrease may reflect formerly "mid-senior" roles being relabeled as "entry" with higher soft expectations (T12 hypothesis)

Resolution needed: The analysis should test whether the YOE decrease is concentrated in specific companies or broad, and whether it correlates with AI keyword presence.

docs.title: 6.2 Field-wide vs. SWE-specific
docs.text: Scope inflation exists at all seniority levels (T12: field-wide), but the entry-level decline is SWE-specific (T16: control group shows opposite trend). These are not contradictory: - Scope inflation is a profession-wide linguistic trend (longer descriptions, more organizational language everywhere) - But the hiring pipeline restructuring (fewer entry-level openings, more AI requirements) is occupation-specific - The claim should be: "SWE is experiencing both general scope inflation AND SWE-specific entry-level contraction"

docs.title: 6.3 Within-company vs. composition
docs.text: The overall entry decline (-3.8pp) is weaker than the within-company decline (-9.3pp) because new 2026 companies partially offset with higher entry shares (+5.6pp composition effect, T13). The analysis should: - Lead with within-company results as the better-identified finding - Note that the composition effect dilutes the aggregate signal - Use the 71-company micro-panel (3+ postings both periods) for company fixed-effects

docs.title: 6.4 AI convergence interpretation
docs.text:
The employer-worker AI gap closed (T14), but this could mean: - Employers genuinely restructured jobs around AI (task-level change) - Employers added AI keywords to postings without changing actual job content (signaling) - AI tool adoption became universal enough that mentioning it is table stakes (institutionalization)

The interview artifacts (T17) are designed to probe which mechanism interviewees perceive.

docs.title: 7. Sensitivity Requirements
docs.text: Every key finding in the analysis phase should be accompanied by the following sensitivity checks:

docs.title: 7.1 Aggregator exclusion
docs.text:
AND is_aggregator = false

Impact: Removes 6,056 asaniczka + 533 arshkon + 610 scraped SWE rows. T06 showed <1.5pp shift in seniority distributions after exclusion.

docs.title: 7.2 Company capping
docs.text: Cap at 10 postings per company per period. T06 showed <1.5pp shift. Report capped results as sensitivity.

docs.title: 7.3 Regex-only SWE classification
docs.text:
AND swe_classification_tier = 'regex'

Drops from 30,283 to ~25,534 SWE rows (84%). Removes the 26% FP rate embedding_llm tier (T04).

docs.title: 7.4 Seniority variant table
docs.text: For every seniority-stratified finding, report a table with at least: - Variant A: seniority_patched (primary) - Variant B: seniority_final (broken, for comparison) - Variant C: seniority_native only (where available)

docs.title: 7.5 Description-length normalization
docs.text: For keyword analyses, report: - Binary indicator (any mention in posting) -- primary - Rate per 1,000 characters -- sensitivity - Show that the finding holds after normalization

docs.title: 7.6 Asaniczka exclusion
docs.text: For any finding that uses asaniczka data, report the arshkon-vs-scraped result separately as the cleaner comparison.

docs.title: 7.7 "Jobs via Dice" exclusion
docs.text: Exclude the 130 scraped rows from "Jobs via Dice" and the 53 from "Turing" in sensitivity.

docs.title: 8.1 [BLOCKING] Stage 5 native_backfill bug
docs.text:
File: preprocessing/scripts/stage5_classification.py, resolve_seniority_final() (line ~624)

Problem: infer_seniority_family() does not align with is_swe classification, causing native_backfill to fire for only 1 of 30,283 SWE rows. 9,490 SWE rows with usable seniority_native labels are being ignored.

Fix: Align infer_seniority_family() with the is_swe classification path so that family = 'swe' is returned for all rows where is_swe = true.

Impact of fix: SWE unknown rate drops from 35% to 4%. Entry-level count increases from 345 to 1,273. The seniority_patched workaround replicates the expected behavior, but the pipeline should be fixed to make seniority_final correct natively.

Workaround available: exploration/seniority_workaround.sql -- use seniority_patched via SQL CTE.

docs.title: 8.2 [HIGH] Run seniority_llm
docs.text:
Action: Run Stage 10/11 with seniority classification task enabled for all SWE + SWE-adjacent + control rows.

Impact: Provides an independent third seniority signal. Resolves the direction disagreement (T09) by classifying the ~20% of rows that remain unknown even after the patched workaround.

docs.title: 8.3 [HIGH] Run swe_classification_llm
docs.text:
Action: Run Stage 10/11 with SWE classification task for embedding_llm tier rows.

Impact: Reduces the 26% FP rate in the embedding_llm tier (T04). Currently ~1,072 false positive SWE rows from hardware/manufacturing roles.

docs.title: 8.4 [MEDIUM] Add "Jobs via Dice" to aggregator list
docs.text:
Action: Add "Jobs via Dice" and "Turing" to Stage 2 aggregator patterns.

Impact: Removes ~183 scraped rows that are aggregator postings not currently flagged.

docs.title: 8.5 [LOW] Stage 12 validation
docs.text:
Action: Run Stage 12 end-to-end validation on a sample before full-batch LLM run.

Impact: Ensures the LLM pipeline produces valid seniority_llm, swe_classification_llm, and ghost_assessment_llm at scale.

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
1. Cross-period comparisons must rely on text-derived features. Structured fields like skills_raw, company_industry, and company_size are not consistently available. The description text is the universal analysis substrate.
  2. Seniority is the weakest link for RQ1. The unknown rate (29-55% for SWE) means seniority-stratified analyses discard a large fraction of data. LLM-based seniority classification (Stage 12) is the highest-impact improvement.
  3. Entry-level baseline is extremely thin. Only 89 arshkon SWE postings have native entry-level labels. The 124 asaniczka "entry" labels are imputed from title keywords, not platform labels.
  4. Geography is moderately available (~56-75% metro_area coverage), sufficient for geographic controls but not for metro-level deep dives on thin seniority slices.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
• Coverage rates are computed after default filters (LinkedIn, English, date_flag=ok), which remove ~60K rows from the 1.22M total.
  • "Non-null" for VARCHAR columns means both non-NULL and non-empty-string.
  • The seniority_final column is always populated (100%) but 72.5% of values are "unknown", which is functionally equivalent to null for analysis purposes.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. Downstream tasks must not assume skills_raw or company_industry are available cross-period. Use description-derived features instead.
  2. Any seniority-stratified analysis must report the effective sample size after excluding unknowns, and discuss the potential bias from non-random missingness.
  3. Stage 12 (LLM seniority) is the single highest-value pipeline improvement for RQ1-RQ3.
  4. For RQ2 keyword/pattern work, prefer description over description_core given the 44% accuracy of boilerplate removal.

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: The seniority label variants produce contradictory RQ1 results: rule-based variants (A, C) show entry-level share increasing from 2024 to 2026, while native-label variants (B, E) show it decreasing. This discrepancy is driven by a pipeline bug where native_backfill effectively does not fire for SWE rows (only 1 of 30,283 SWE rows gets backfilled), leaving 9,490 SWE rows with available seniority_native labels unused. The rule-based classifier has excellent mid-senior recall (98%) but poor entry (30-61%) and associate (8-15%) recall, systematically underclassifying junior roles.

docs.title: seniority_imputed vs seniority_final (SWE)
docs.text: • 100% agreement, kappa = 1.0 -- these columns are identical for SWE rows, confirming native_backfill is not functioning.

docs.title: seniority_native vs seniority_final (SWE, both non-null/unknown)
docs.text:
• 18,754 rows evaluable
  • 93.3% raw agreement, Cohen's kappa = 0.34 (fair)
  • High raw agreement is inflated by the mid-senior base rate (~92% of evaluable rows). Kappa reveals poor agreement beyond chance.

docs.title: seniority_native vs seniority_imputed (SWE, both non-null, imputed non-unknown)
docs.text: • Identical to native vs final (confirming the bug).

docs.title: Per-class accuracy (rule-based classifier vs native ground truth)
docs.text:
Class arshkon (2024-04) scraped (2026-03) asaniczka (2024-01) entry 29.6% recall 60.9% recall N/A (no labels) associate 8.1% recall 9.2% recall 15.2% recall mid-senior 98.4% recall 98.3% recall 98.5% recall director 16.7% recall 17.8% recall N/A Overall 86.2% 89.1% 94.6%

Key observations: - Mid-senior recall is stable at ~98% across all sources and periods. - Entry recall is substantially higher in scraped (60.9%) than arshkon (29.6%), suggesting the 2026 postings have more explicit seniority signals in titles. - Associate and director recall are consistently poor (<20%).

docs.title: Native backfill bug
docs.text:
resolve_seniority_final() in stage5_classification.py (line 624) is designed to backfill from seniority_native when the rule-based classifier returns "unknown". However, it only does so when infer_seniority_family() returns "swe", "adjacent", or "control". The infer_seniority_family() function uses swe_lookup through normalize_swe_title_key(), while is_swe is set via a different path through title_normalized. This mismatch causes many rows with is_swe = true to have family = "other", bypassing native_backfill.

Impact of the bug on SWE seniority distribution:

Seniority Current count With backfill fix Multiplier entry 345 1,273 3.7x associate 290 1,623 5.6x mid-senior 18,885 26,103 1.4x director 117 128 1.1x unknown 10,646 1,156 0.1x

Unknown rate drops from 35.2% to 3.8% with the fix.

docs.title: RQ1 Junior share by variant
docs.text:
Variant 2024-01 2024-04 2026-03 Direction A: seniority_final (non-unknown) 0.81% 4.42% 5.65% INCREASING B: seniority_native only 0.00%* 22.91% 15.49% DECREASING C: high-confidence (title_keyword + native_backfill) 0.88% 5.06% 6.67% INCREASING E: hypothetical with backfill fix 0.57% 18.69% 12.28% DECREASING

*asaniczka has zero entry-level native labels

Variants A and C show entry share increasing over time, suggesting expanding entry-level hiring. Variants B and E (which include native labels) show entry share decreasing from 2024-04 to 2026-03, suggesting shrinking entry-level share. The direction of the RQ1 finding depends entirely on which seniority operationalization is used.

The discrepancy arises because: 1. The rule-based classifier misses ~70% of entry-level postings (by not recognizing entry-level titles without explicit "junior/intern/entry" keywords). 2. The native label captures entry-level postings that have generic titles like "Software Engineer" but platform-assigned entry seniority. 3. The 2024-04 period (arshkon) has much richer native entry labels than 2026-03 (scraped), creating a measurement artifact.

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
RQ1 results are NOT ROBUST to seniority operationalization. This is a blocking issue. Until the native_backfill bug is fixed AND the LLM seniority classifier runs, RQ1 conclusions about junior-share trends should be considered provisional. The rule-based classifier alone cannot reliably answer RQ1 because it systematically undercounts entry-level postings.

Recommended priority: 1. Fix the native_backfill bug in Stage 5 (align infer_seniority_family with is_swe classification logic). 2. Run seniority_llm at scale to provide a third independent signal. 3. Report RQ1 using multiple variants with explicit uncertainty bounds.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
• asaniczka (2024-01) has zero entry-level native labels, making it impossible to use native-only analysis for the largest period.
  • arshkon's 89 native entry-level SWE postings are the sole historical entry-level anchor.
  • Cohen's kappa of 0.34 indicates the classifier and native labels have only "fair" agreement beyond what chance would predict, concentrated in non-mid-senior classes.
  • Scraped data has higher entry recall (61% vs 30%) than arshkon, suggesting classifier performance varies with data vintage.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. [BLOCKING] Fix native_backfill bug in Stage 5. The infer_seniority_family() function must align with the is_swe classification path so that SWE rows get backfilled. This alone would reduce SWE unknown rate from 35% to 4%.
  2. [HIGH] Run seniority_llm. The LLM classifier is the best path to resolving the entry/associate undercount without relying solely on platform native labels.
  3. [HIGH] Report RQ1 with variant sensitivity table. Any RQ1 finding must show results under at least variants A and E (or the backfill-fixed equivalent).
  4. [MEDIUM] Investigate why scraped has 2x better entry recall than arshkon. This could indicate title wording changes between 2024 and 2026, or it could indicate selection bias in the scraper's query design.
  5. [LOW] Consider if associate/director misclassification matters for RQ1. RQ1 focuses on junior share, and associate recall (~10%) is poor but associate-to-mid-senior confusion may be less consequential if the 3-level collapse (junior/mid/senior) is used.

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: Missingness patterns are strongly source-driven rather than random. Asaniczka (1.01M rows, 88% of the filtered dataset) has zero coverage for company_industry, company_size, work_type, and skills_raw -- but 100% seniority_native (only mid-senior and associate). Arshkon has the richest structured metadata but the smallest SWE sample (4,438). The scraped 2026 data has good coverage on most fields but almost no date_posted (0.4%). The most consequential gap is seniority: after excluding "unknown", effective SWE sample sizes drop to 2,015 (arshkon), 15,286 (asaniczka), and 2,336 (scraped).

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
1. Cross-period seniority comparisons use 19,637 of 30,283 SWE rows (65%), and the composition of "known" seniority differs by source: asaniczka contributes zero native entry-level labels, while arshkon and scraped contribute the only entry-level baseline.
  2. The associate/entry overlap is extensive. 60 title strings appear as both "associate" in asaniczka and "entry" in arshkon, covering the majority of entry-level volume. This means asaniczka's "associate" bin likely contains what arshkon would label "entry". Treating associate and entry as distinct levels across sources is unreliable without LLM re-classification.
  3. Structured skills data cannot support cross-period analysis. skills_raw is 99.9% populated for asaniczka but 1.3% for arshkon and 0% for scraped. Description-text NLP is the only viable approach for skill/requirement analysis across periods.

docs.text:
Source entry associate mid-senior director intern executive NULL kaggle_arshkon 34,378 (29.1%) 9,421 (8.0%) 39,580 (33.5%) 3,591 (3.0%) 1,359 (1.2%) 1,179 (1.0%) 28,657 (24.2%) kaggle_asaniczka 0 (0%) 114,687 (11.3%) 900,065 (88.7%) 0 0 0 0 scraped 6,660 (25.1%) 1,231 (4.6%) 13,065 (49.2%) 948 (3.6%) 595 (2.2%) 207 (0.8%) 3,870 (14.6%)

Key observation: Asaniczka has exactly two seniority_native values (mid-senior and associate) with zero nulls. This is a platform/scrape artifact, not a reflection of the actual job market. It means: - All asaniczka entry-level postings are labeled either "associate" or "mid-senior" - Asaniczka cannot contribute to entry-level share calculations using native labels - The seniority_final imputation recovers 124 "entry" labels for asaniczka SWE via title keywords, but this is a tiny fraction

docs.title: seniority_native vs seniority_final (SWE, entry-level)
docs.text:
Source seniority_native = 'entry' (SWE) seniority_final = 'entry' (SWE) Source of final entry labels kaggle_arshkon 693 89 Mostly title_keyword imputation; native entry is available but seniority_final uses stricter logic kaggle_asaniczka 0 124 Entirely from title_keyword imputation -- no native entry labels exist scraped 477 132 Mix of native_backfill and title_keyword

The large gap between seniority_native entry counts and seniority_final entry counts (693 vs 89 for arshkon) reflects the stricter Stage 5 resolution logic, which only promotes to "entry" when strong title signals are present.

docs.title: Associate/entry-level title overlap
docs.text:
60 title strings appear as both "associate" (seniority_native) in asaniczka and "entry" (seniority_native) in arshkon SWE rows. The top overlapping titles:

Title asaniczka associate count arshkon entry count software engineer 105 52 systems engineer 48 19 data engineer 32 28 automation engineer 33 16 test engineer 39 9 java developer 22 18 quality assurance engineer 26 6 software developer 19 12 developer 5 23 product engineer 19 8

These are generic SWE titles that LinkedIn labels at different seniority levels across the two datasets. This confirms that asaniczka's "associate" category substantially overlaps with arshkon's "entry" category. Any analysis comparing entry-level shares across periods must account for this label inconsistency.

Additionally, within arshkon alone, many SWE titles appear at multiple seniority levels. For example, "software engineer" has 248 mid-senior, 52 entry, and 18 associate postings. "automation engineer" has 17 mid-senior, 16 entry, and 4 associate. This means the same title string is not a reliable proxy for seniority level.

docs.title: Entry-level effective sample sizes for RQ1
docs.text:
The binding constraint for RQ1 (junior share analysis) is entry-level volume:

Metric arshkon (2024-04) asaniczka (2024-01) scraped (2026-03) Total SWE (filtered) 4,438 21,626 4,219 seniority_final = entry 89 124 132 seniority_native = entry 693 0 477 Entry share (seniority_final) 2.0% 0.6% 3.1%

The 0.6% asaniczka entry share is an artifact of title-keyword imputation only (no native labels). The arshkon 2.0% and scraped 3.1% are more comparable but still rest on small samples (89 and 132 rows).

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
1. Missingness is not random. Asaniczka's zero coverage on company_industry, company_size, and work_type is structural (those fields were not in the original Kaggle dataset). Similarly, skills_raw being asaniczka-only reflects their companion skills file.
  2. The seniority_final "unknown" rate varies by source, from 29% (asaniczka) to 55% (arshkon). Analyses excluding unknowns will over-represent asaniczka and under-represent arshkon in the pooled sample.
  3. Metro_area resolution varies: 66% for arshkon, 75% for asaniczka/scraped. Geographic analyses lose 25-34% of SWE rows.
  4. yoe_extracted is sparse but comparably sparse across sources (16-40%), so cross-period comparisons of YOE distributions are feasible, conditional on the extraction succeeding.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. Do not treat asaniczka "associate" as equivalent to arshkon "associate". Asaniczka "associate" likely includes what other sources would label "entry". Cross-period seniority analysis should either:
  2. Use only arshkon + scraped (both have native entry labels), accepting the smaller sample
  3. Wait for LLM seniority classification (Stage 12) to re-label all sources consistently
  4. Use title-based seniority proxies as a robustness check
  5. Report entry-level counts alongside any junior-share statistics. With n=89 (arshkon) and n=132 (scraped), confidence intervals will be wide.
  6. For cross-period analysis, the viable common field set is: period, is_swe, seniority_final (with caveats), description, title_normalized, company_name_effective, state_normalized, metro_area (partial), is_remote_inferred, yoe_extracted (sparse).
  7. Do not use skills_raw, company_industry, company_size, or work_type for cross-period comparisons. Extract these from description text instead.
  8. Downstream T-tasks that stratify by seniority must document their effective n per cell and flag any cell with n < 100 as unreliable.

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: The SWE classification is reliable for the regex tier (84% of SWE rows, ~2% estimated FP rate) but has a 26% false positive rate in the embedding_llm tier (14% of SWE rows). The primary FP pattern is hardware/physical test engineers and manufacturing automation engineers being classified as software engineers. Overall estimated FP rate is ~5.5% (~1,677 of 30,283 SWE rows). False negative rate among postings with SWE-like titles is ~12%, primarily missing roles like "Elixir Engineer", "GUI Developer", and "Python Developer" that lack standard SWE title keywords.

docs.text: Tier Count % of SWE Est. FP Rate Est. FP Count regex 25,534 84.3% ~2% ~511 embedding_llm 4,123 13.6% ~26% ~1,072 embedding_high 626 2.1% ~15% ~94 Total 30,283 100% ~5.5% ~1,677

docs.title: Borderline SWE audit (embedding_llm tier, n=50)
docs.text:
From 50 randomly sampled embedding_llm tier SWE postings: - 33 (66%) are true SWE (Java developers, .NET developers, full-stack developers, etc.) - 4 (8%) are borderline (MLOps engineers, QA leads in non-software contexts) - 13 (26%) are false positives (not primarily software development)

Common false positive patterns in embedding_llm tier: 1. Hardware test engineers (e.g., "Test Engineer" for opto-electronics, LabVIEW hardware test, semiconductor test) 2. Manufacturing reliability/automation (e.g., "Reliability Engineer" for fixed equipment, "Automation Engineer" for PLC controls) 3. Quality assurance in manufacturing (e.g., "Quality Assurance Engineer" for production line) 4. Firmware engineers in hardware-primary contexts (e.g., well services equipment)

docs.title: Borderline non-SWE audit (titles with engineer/developer/software, n=50)
docs.text:
From 50 randomly sampled non-SWE postings with SWE-like title keywords: - 34 (68%) are true negatives (civil engineers, electrical engineers, manufacturing engineers, etc.) - 10 (20%) are borderline (cybersecurity, network engineering, robotics) - 6 (12%) are false negatives (should have been classified as SWE)

False negative examples: - "Elixir Engineer, Global Cart Team" -- clearly a programming role - "GUI Developer" -- software GUI development - "Python Developer" -- standard software development - "Manager of Software Engineering" -- software management role - "React Native developer" -- mobile development (classified as adjacent, arguable) - "Software Engineering Team Lead" -- SE leadership (classified as adjacent, arguable)

docs.title: Regex tier quality
docs.text: The regex tier (84% of SWE rows) is high-quality. Top titles include: Software Engineer (2,373), Data Engineer (861), Systems Engineer (720), Software Developer (349), DevOps Engineer (335). However, "Systems Engineer" (720 rows) includes some ambiguous cases (e.g., "Senior Electrical Systems Engineer", "Sr. AV Systems Engineer") that are hardware-focused. Estimated FP rate in regex tier is ~2%, concentrated in the "Systems Engineer" and "Product Engineer" titles.

docs.title: SWE-adjacent profile (8,486 rows)
docs.text:
Top titles: Network Engineer (937), Quality Engineer (863), Data Analyst (595), Architect (535), Solutions Architect (197). This group contains technical roles that involve some code or technical design but are not primarily software development. The classification is reasonable -- these roles are appropriate for RQ2 cross-occupation comparisons.

All SWE-adjacent rows come from embedding-based tiers (5,189 embedding_adjacent, 3,297 embedding_llm), which is expected since regex patterns target SWE-specific titles.

docs.title: Control group profile (125,995 rows)
docs.text:
Top titles: Accountant (5,132), Registered Nurse (3,207), Electrical Engineer (1,928), Accounting Manager (1,634), Mechanical Engineer (1,429). The control group is a mix of non-technical (accounting, nursing) and technical-but-not-software (electrical, mechanical, civil engineering) occupations. This provides a good baseline for cross-occupation comparisons in RQ1.

Note: 6 rows are flagged as both is_swe_adjacent and is_control (likely a minor classification edge case). 2 rows are flagged as both is_swe and is_control.

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text: SWE classification is adequate for RQ1-RQ3 analysis with the following caveats: 1. The 5.5% FP rate introduces noise but should not systematically bias period comparisons (FP rate is similar across sources/periods). 2. For sensitivity analysis, restricting to regex tier only (84% of SWE rows) provides a cleaner sample at the cost of smaller n. 3. The embedding_llm tier should be used with caution -- its 26% FP rate means ~1 in 4 rows in this tier is not actually a SWE role. 4. False negatives (missed SWE postings) are concentrated among roles with unconventional titles ("Elixir Engineer", "GUI Developer") and are likely a small fraction of the total SWE population.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
• SWE confidence scores are not granular within tiers: regex = 1.0, embedding_llm = 0.9, embedding_high = 0.85-0.99. There is no useful confidence gradient for within-tier filtering.
  • The 0.3-0.7 confidence range specified in the task contains zero SWE rows (all non-SWE). The confidence score is binary-like rather than continuous.
  • The "Systems Engineer" title (720 SWE rows) is the largest source of ambiguity in the regex tier, containing a mix of software systems engineering and hardware/electrical systems engineering.
  • SWE-adjacent classification boundary is reasonable but includes roles like "Data Analyst" that some researchers would include in a broader SWE definition.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. [HIGH] Run swe_classification_llm at scale. The LLM classifier should substantially reduce FP rate in the embedding_llm tier by reading descriptions, not just titles. This is the most impactful improvement path.
  2. [MEDIUM] Consider regex-tier-only sensitivity analysis for key RQ1/RQ2 findings. Report main results on full SWE sample, then show robustness check on regex-only.
  3. [MEDIUM] Review "Systems Engineer" regex pattern. Consider whether title-only regex should require software/computing context words alongside "Systems Engineer", or whether these should be reclassified to adjacent.
  4. [LOW] Investigate the 8 rows with dual is_swe+is_control or is_swe_adjacent+is_control flags. These are likely minor edge cases but should not exist by design.
  5. [LOW] Consider adding "Elixir", "GUI Developer", "Python Developer" patterns to the regex SWE title list to reduce false negatives on clearly-SWE roles with unconventional titles.

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: The three datasets show statistically significant distributional differences across nearly every dimension tested, but the differences are moderate in practical magnitude and largely attributable to known structural factors rather than measurement artifacts. Description lengths grew ~50% from 2024 to 2026 (median 3,124 -> 3,948 -> 5,110 chars), consistent with real posting inflation. Company overlap is low (Jaccard 0.08-0.12) but top-50 overlap is reasonable (20-32%), reflecting natural employer turnover plus different dataset sizes. Geographic distributions are broadly stable (CA, TX, VA dominate in all three periods), with Cramer's V = 0.14-0.18 indicating small effect sizes. The critical finding is that seniority distributions are nearly identical between arshkon and scraped (chi2 p=0.096, not significant) but sharply different from asaniczka (chi2 p<1e-55), confirming asaniczka's missing entry-level labels as a structural gap rather than a temporal shift.

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
RQ1 (junior share): The arshkon-vs-scraped comparison is the only valid two-period seniority comparison. Entry-level share is 4.4% in arshkon (2024-04, n=89) and 5.7% in scraped (2026-03, n=132). Asaniczka cannot contribute to entry-level trend analysis due to its systematic zero entry-level coverage. Any RQ1 analysis must flag the arshkon-only historical baseline and the thin n=89 anchor.

RQ2 (task migration): Description length inflation (50% growth) means raw keyword counts must be normalized by description length or use rate-per-posting measures. The description_core field amplifies this problem (core_length KS D=0.39 for asaniczka vs scraped), likely because boilerplate removal is inconsistent across periods. Prefer description for keyword work.

RQ3 (employer-requirement divergence): Company turnover across periods (Jaccard ~0.10) means cross-period requirement comparisons should focus on the ~400-670 overlapping companies or use industry/size strata rather than assuming company-level panel continuity.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
1. KS tests are all significant (p < 1e-14 for every pairwise comparison on every dimension), but this is expected given sample sizes of 4K-22K. Effect sizes (KS D-statistics, Cramer's V) are the interpretively meaningful quantities.

  2. Industry labels are not comparable across sources. Scraped data uses multi-industry compound labels (e.g., "Accounting, Financial Services, and IT Services and IT Consulting") while arshkon uses single-industry labels. The industry chi-squared (V=0.34) overstates real difference because it conflates label format with industry composition. Industry comparisons require label harmonization before analysis.

  3. Asaniczka seniority caveat. Asaniczka's seniority distribution (97.6% mid-senior among known) is an artifact of having only mid-senior and associate native labels. This is documented in the schema but bears repeating: asaniczka should be excluded from seniority-stratified analyses or used only for mid-senior subgroup comparisons.

  4. Title vocabulary overlap is very low (Jaccard 0.04-0.06). This partly reflects how LinkedIn title strings work (minor phrasing differences create distinct normalized titles). The unique-to-scraped titles show AI-era patterns ("ai data engineer", "ai full stack engineer manager", "go/golang software engineer - ai training") that do not appear in 2024 data.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. Normalize keyword/pattern counts by description length in RQ2 analysis to avoid confounding real requirement changes with posting length inflation.
  2. Harmonize industry labels before any industry-stratified analysis. Scraped compound labels need splitting or mapping to single-industry categories.
  3. Use arshkon as the sole historical anchor for entry-level analysis. Document the n=89 entry-level SWE baseline prominently in limitations.
  4. Use description (not description_core) for cross-period keyword analysis. The core_length distributions diverge much more than description_length for asaniczka vs scraped (D=0.39 vs D=0.20), suggesting boilerplate removal introduces period-specific artifacts.
  5. Consider restricting cross-period company analyses to the ~380 overlapping companies between arshkon and scraped for within-company temporal comparisons.

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: Company concentration is moderate and varies substantially across datasets, but no single employer dominates enough to bias overall findings. Asaniczka is the most concentrated (HHI=0.015, Gini=0.71, top-20 share=33%) due to Capital One (7.8%) and Recruiting from Scratch (7.5%) together accounting for 15% of SWE postings. Arshkon and scraped are less concentrated (HHI ~0.003-0.004, Gini 0.46-0.55, top-20 share 17-23%). The critical robustness result: capping at 10 postings per company barely moves seniority shares -- junior share shifts by at most 1 percentage point in any dataset, confirming that seniority findings are not driven by a few high-volume employers. Within-company seniority distributions for the 43 companies present in both arshkon and scraped are virtually identical (entry: 3.7% vs 3.7%; mid-senior: 93.9% vs 94.0%), suggesting no detectable within-company seniority shift between 2024 and 2026 among these employers.

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
RQ1 (junior share): The company-capped sensitivity test provides strong evidence that junior-share estimates are robust to employer concentration. The maximum shift from capping is +1.0pp for scraped (5.7% -> 6.7%) and +0.4pp for arshkon (4.4% -> 4.7%). This means any cross-period junior-share comparison is not an artifact of a few companies posting disproportionately at one seniority level.

RQ1 (within-company trends): The 43 overlap companies show no within-company seniority shift. This null result is important: if the aggregate junior share changes across periods, it would be driven by compositional change (different companies entering/exiting the market) rather than within-company restructuring. However, the overlap sample (n=379 arshkon, n=519 scraped with known seniority) is small and dominated by mid-senior labels, so this null result has limited power to detect a real effect.

RQ2/RQ3: The five dominant companies (Capital One, ClearanceJobs, DataAnnotation, Jobs via Dice, Recruiting from Scratch) are concentrated in specific datasets and should be examined individually in sensitivity analysis. Capital One alone is 7.8% of asaniczka SWE postings; any requirement pattern common across Capital One postings will appear as a population-level pattern in asaniczka.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
1. Dominant companies differ by dataset. DataAnnotation tops arshkon (3.7%), Capital One + Recruiting from Scratch dominate asaniczka (15.3% combined), and Jobs via Dice tops scraped (3.1%). The fact that dominant employers are dataset-specific (and include aggregators like Dice and Recruiting from Scratch) means aggregator handling in Stage 2 is a consequential preprocessing choice.

  2. "Jobs via Dice" in scraped suggests residual aggregator leakage. This entity being the top SWE employer in scraped data (130 postings, 3.1%) indicates Stage 2 aggregator detection may not fully resolve Dice-aggregated postings. These should be examined to see if real_employer was extracted.

  3. Asaniczka concentration is anomalously high. Its Gini (0.71) is well above arshkon (0.46) and scraped (0.55). This may reflect asaniczka's scraping methodology (search-based collection that over-represents certain companies) rather than the actual labor market. Analysis should report asaniczka results with and without Capital One / Recruiting from Scratch.

  4. Overlap company sample is biased toward large, stable employers. The 43 companies appearing in both arshkon and scraped (with >=5 postings each) are survivorship-biased toward firms that were hiring in both 2024 and 2026. This is appropriate for within-company comparison but should not be generalized as representative of all SWE employers.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. Include company-capped results as a standard robustness check in all seniority analyses. The shift is small (<1.5pp) but should be reported.
  2. Investigate "Jobs via Dice" aggregator residual. Check whether Stage 2 extracted real_employer for these 130 scraped postings. If not, consider adding Dice-specific extraction rules.
  3. Run asaniczka analyses with and without Capital One + Recruiting from Scratch (15.3% of SWE postings). Their removal tests whether requirement patterns are population-level or company-specific.
  4. Flag DataAnnotation in arshkon. DataAnnotation (3.7% of arshkon SWE) is a data-labeling company that may post systematically different SWE roles. Verify these are genuine SWE postings and not data-annotation tasks miscategorized.
  5. Within-company null result needs caveats. The identical seniority distributions (3.7% entry in both periods) come from just 43 companies with only 14 and 19 entry-level postings respectively. This is consistent with no change but lacks statistical power to rule out meaningful shifts.

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: All three datasets achieve strong geographic representativeness against BLS OES state-level employment for SOC 15-1252 (Software Developers). Pearson r ranges from 0.96 to 0.98 (linear) across datasets, well above the 0.80 target. The combined dataset achieves r=0.978 across all 50 states+DC. Industry representativeness is moderate (Spearman rho=0.70), with systematic overrepresentation of Information-sector postings (+9.3pp) and underrepresentation of Professional/Technical Services (-18.5pp) -- consistent with LinkedIn's skew toward tech-branded companies versus IT consulting embedded in non-tech firms. JOLTS and Indeed benchmarks confirm that all three data collection periods fall within a persistent post-2022 SWE hiring trough, with the Indeed Software Development posting index flat at roughly 70 (vs. a Feb 2020 baseline of 100) across all three periods.

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
RQ1-RQ3 (cross-period comparisons): The high geographic correlations (all r>0.93) mean state-level variation in our data reliably tracks actual SWE employment concentration. Cross-period comparisons of geographic hiring patterns are well-grounded.

Industry bias caveat: The Professional/Technical Services sector -- the largest SWE employer (41.8% of BLS employment) -- is underrepresented at 23.3% of our postings. This sector includes IT consulting, staffing firms, and contract SWE roles. Analyses of industry composition should note this gap. The overrepresentation of Information (29.3% vs 20.0%) means pure-play tech firms are overweighted.

Temporal context: The JOLTS Information sector series shows volatile month-to-month swings (98K-177K range in 2024 alone), so monthly snapshots carry noise. However, the Indeed SWE index -- a more granular and less volatile metric -- shows all three periods clustered tightly between 69.9 and 71.9, confirming they sample from essentially the same labor market regime. The 2024-to-2026 comparison is therefore not confounded by a major hiring cycle shift.

Scraped data geographic coverage: The scraped dataset covers only 30 states (vs. 47-50 for Kaggle sources) due to the 26-metro search frame, producing a high linear r (0.98) but lower log-space r (0.78) driven by missing small states. This is by design, not a bias concern, but geographic analyses should note the narrower coverage.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
1. Industry mapping is approximate. LinkedIn company_industry labels do not map cleanly to NAICS sectors. 17.3% of industry labels in our data (arshkon+scraped) could not be mapped, mostly compound multi-industry labels from scraped LinkedIn data (e.g., "Accounting, Financial Services, and Business Consulting and Services"). Most unmapped labels belong in Professional/Technical Services or Information, so the true gap between our data and OES is likely smaller than reported.

  2. OES is employment, our data is postings. OES measures total employment stocks (May 2024 survey); our data measures job posting flows on LinkedIn. These are conceptually different: posting volume reflects hiring activity, not installed base. High correlation confirms geographic alignment but does not validate volume levels.

  3. SOC 15-1256 retired. The May 2024 OES no longer publishes SOC 15-1256 (the combined Software Developers/QA code). The analysis uses 15-1252 (Software Developers) as the sole benchmark, which aligns well with our is_swe classification.

  4. JOLTS is sector-level, not occupation-level. JOLTS Information sector covers all occupations in NAICS 51, not just SWE. It serves as a directional context indicator, not a direct benchmark.

  5. Vermont (VT) has suppressed OES data (reported as "**" in the source). This state is excluded from the correlation with zero imputation.

  6. Scraped daily counts are limited. Only 2 scraping days (Mar 20-21) are in the deduped stage8 dataset, so daily-rate comparisons with JOLTS monthly figures are illustrative only.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. Geographic weighting not needed. The r>0.93 correlations across all datasets confirm that geographic representativeness is adequate for unweighted cross-state analysis. No poststratification required.

  2. Industry sensitivity analysis recommended. For RQ1/RQ2 analyses that touch industry composition, run a sensitivity check excluding Staffing/Recruiting (aggregator proxy) and reweighting to OES sector shares. The Professional Services underrepresentation could affect results about contract-vs-direct hiring patterns.

  3. Note scraped state coverage in methods. The 30-state coverage of scraped data is by design (metro-targeted search) and should be disclosed. For state-level temporal comparisons (2024 vs 2026), restrict to the 30 overlapping states.

  4. JOLTS/Indeed context for paper framing. The Indeed SWE index at ~70 (vs. peak of ~229 in Feb 2022) confirms our data captures a sustained post-pandemic hiring contraction. All three collection windows sample from a flat-to-declining SWE demand floor. This context should appear in the paper's methods section.

  5. Unmapped industry labels (17.3%). A future refinement could parse compound LinkedIn industry labels into component NAICS sectors. This is low priority given the moderate overall industry correlation (rho=0.70).

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: SWE job postings lengthened substantially: median description grew from 3,124 chars (2024-04) to 5,110 chars (2026-03), a 64% increase. The entry-level share (using seniority_patched) rose from 0.6% (2024-01, asaniczka) to 21.0% (2024-04, arshkon) to 13.9% (2026-03, scraped). However, the asaniczka 0.6% figure is misleading because asaniczka has no native entry-level labels -- its entry share is purely from title-keyword detection. Comparing arshkon to scraped (both with native labels), the entry share dropped from 21.0% to 13.9%. Meanwhile, remote work rose from effectively 0% to 24.7%, and aggregator rates dropped from 28.0% to 12-14%.

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
1. RQ1 (junior share): The arshkon-to-scraped comparison shows entry share declining from 21.0% to 13.9% (-7.1pp). This is the most apples-to-apples comparison since both sources have native entry-level labels. Asaniczka cannot contribute to entry-level trend analysis due to its labeling gap.
  2. Description length growth (64% median increase) means all text-based analyses must normalize by document length. Raw keyword counts would be biased upward for 2026-03.
  3. YOE drop (median 5 -> 3 years) in 2026-03 is directionally consistent with more junior postings, but coverage dropped from 35-40% to 16%, so this may be non-representative.
  4. Remote work comparisons across periods are not viable due to Kaggle data collection gaps.
  5. Aggregator rate difference (28% vs 12-14%) warrants sensitivity analysis with aggregators excluded.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
1. Asaniczka's seniority distribution is an artifact: 100% native coverage but zero entry-level labels produces 0.56% entry share (purely from title keyword detection). Do not include asaniczka in entry-share trend analysis.
  2. YOE extraction coverage dropped substantially in 2026-03 (16.4% vs 34-40%). YOE-based analyses have selection bias.
  3. Remote flag is systematically missing for Kaggle sources -- treat the cross-period remote comparison as data artifact, not finding.
  4. The seniority patched workaround reduces unknown from 29-55% to 0-20%, but the asaniczka 0% unknown rate is because all rows get native backfill (all mid-senior/associate). This creates a mechanical dominance of mid-senior in asaniczka.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. For RQ1 analyses: Use arshkon vs scraped as the primary comparison pair. Asaniczka provides volume context but not entry-level trend signal.
  2. Normalize text metrics: All keyword counts, n-grams, and text features must be normalized by description length to avoid systematic 2026-03 bias.
  3. Run aggregator-excluded sensitivity: The 28% vs 12-14% aggregator rate difference could shift seniority distributions.
  4. Remote analysis: Either drop cross-period remote comparisons or only report the 2026-03 level descriptively.
  5. YOE findings are suggestive but not primary: Low coverage in 2026-03 limits reliability. Use as supporting evidence, not standalone finding.

docs.title: Finding
docs.text:
The junior-share finding is not robust across seniority operationalizations. The arshkon-to-scraped direction flips depending on whether native platform labels are included:

  • Variants with native labels (A, C, D): Entry share decreased by 7.1-7.4pp (from 21-23% to 14-15%). All three agree on magnitude (-32% to -34% relative change).
  • Variants without native labels (B, F): Entry share increased by 1.2-1.6pp (from 4-5% to 6-7%). Both agree on magnitude (+28% to +32% relative change).

The native-label cluster and the title-only cluster are internally consistent but disagree on direction. This is the same blocker identified in Gate 1 (T02).

docs.title: Variant Details
docs.text: Variant Description Arshkon entry % Scraped entry % Change (pp) Change (%) Direction Entry n (arshkon/scraped) A: Patched (primary) Workaround: title_strong > native > weak > unknown 20.97 13.91 -7.06 -33.7% Decreasing 745 / 549 B: seniority_final (broken) Pipeline output with native_backfill bug 4.42 5.65 +1.23 +27.8% Increasing 89 / 132 C: Native only Platform-provided labels where available 22.91 15.49 -7.42 -32.4% Decreasing 710 / 545 D: High-confidence title_strong + native_backfill only 21.45 14.20 -7.25 -33.8% Decreasing 745 / 548 F: Title-strong only Title keyword/manager classification only 5.06 6.67 +1.61 +31.8% Increasing 87 / 130

docs.title: Why the direction flips
docs.text:
The disagreement is mechanical, not statistical:

  1. Native labels capture entry-level that titles miss. Arshkon has 693 native entry-level labels vs 87 title-detected. The title-based classifier has ~30-61% recall for entry-level (T02 finding). Variants A/C/D include the native-detected entry-level postings; B/F do not.

  2. Coverage differs by period. Arshkon has 70% native coverage; scraped has 83% native coverage. Both have similar title-strong coverage (~39% vs ~46%). The denominator shift interacts with the numerator.

  3. The broken pipeline (B) is doubly biased. It misses native labels for SWE (the bug) AND has higher unknown rates (55% arshkon, 45% scraped), meaning its "known" pool is selected on title-keyword detectability.

docs.title: Agreement assessment
docs.text:
• Same direction? NO. The 5 variants split 3-2.
  • Magnitude within 2x? Within each cluster, yes. The native-label cluster spans -7.06 to -7.42pp (range < 0.5pp). The title-only cluster spans +1.23 to +1.61pp (range < 0.4pp). But across clusters, the magnitudes are not comparable.
  • Which cluster is more trustworthy? The native-label cluster (A, C, D) is based on 3-7x more entry-level observations (545-745 vs 87-132) and has much higher coverage (80-100% vs 45-55% known). The title-only cluster has severe selection bias because it detects only a fraction of entry-level postings.

docs.title: Asaniczka note
docs.text: Asaniczka (2024-01) is excluded from the trend comparison because it has zero native entry-level labels. Its entry shares (0.0%-0.88% depending on variant) are artifacts of title-keyword detection only and do not represent the true entry-level share. Cross-period trends must use arshkon vs scraped.

docs.title: Figures
docs.text:
• ../figures/T09/T09_fig1_sensitivity_comparison.png -- Entry share across periods for all 5 variants (left) and arshkon-to-scraped pp change (right)
  • ../figures/T09/T09_fig2_arshkon_vs_scraped.png -- Side-by-side arshkon vs scraped entry share with sample sizes annotated

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
1. RQ1 direction remains uncertain without seniority_llm. The native-label variants provide a stronger signal (more observations, higher coverage) and suggest entry share is declining. But until the LLM seniority classifier runs, we cannot rule out that native label coverage differences between arshkon and scraped are biasing the result.

  2. The patched workaround (Variant A) is the best available estimate. It has the broadest coverage, uses the strongest signals first, and agrees closely with the native-only and high-confidence variants. It should be the primary reporting variable.

  3. Variant B (broken pipeline) should not be used for primary analysis. It misses most entry-level postings and its direction (increasing) is driven by small-sample noise (89 vs 132 entry-level observations).

  4. The LLM seniority classifier (seniority_llm) is critical for resolving this. It would provide an independent classification for the ~20% of rows that remain unknown even after the patched workaround, and would validate or challenge the native label patterns.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
1. The direction disagreement is a known, expected consequence of the native_backfill bug and the low recall of title-based entry-level detection. It is not a data corruption issue.

  2. Sample sizes for the title-only variants (B, F) are thin: 87-132 entry-level postings. These are not powered to detect changes smaller than ~3pp.

  3. The native-label variants have much stronger statistical power (545-745 entry observations) but their validity depends on the assumption that LinkedIn's seniority labeling process did not change systematically between 2024 and 2026.

  4. Asaniczka contributes nothing to the entry-level trend analysis regardless of variant. It is useful only for mid-senior level questions.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. Use Variant A (patched) as primary, report B (broken) as sensitivity. The patched variant is better-powered and better-grounded.
  2. Flag the direction disagreement in all RQ1 reporting. Until seniority_llm resolves this, state the finding conditionally: "Conditional on native platform labels reflecting true seniority, entry share declined 7pp (-34%). Title-only classification suggests a possible increase of 1-2pp but is underpowered."
  3. Prioritize the Stage 5 bugfix and seniority_llm run. This is the highest-value data improvement for RQ1.
  4. Investigate whether LinkedIn's seniority labeling methodology changed between 2024 and 2026. If the platform's own labeling shifted, native labels would introduce temporal confounding.
  5. For downstream analyses using seniority: always report the number of entry-level observations to make clear when results rest on thin samples.

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: Fightin' Words analysis across 6 corpus pairs reveals three key patterns. (1) AI/ML terminology is the single strongest temporal distinguisher: "ai" is the #1 term in all temporal comparisons (z=-26.8 for junior, z=-43.8 for senior), with 14 AI-related terms significantly distinguishing 2026 junior postings from 2024 and 19 for senior. Specific tooling terms (llm, agents, rag, copilot, langchain) dominate 2026 postings. (2) Ownership/autonomy language shifted toward 2026 (ownership z=-11.9 for senior, autonomous z=-8.8), while traditional management language ("management" z=+14.7) favors 2024. (3) Comparison 5 (Junior 2026 vs Senior 2024) shows 2026 entry-level postings are more AI-heavy than 2024 senior postings (ai z=+38.8), but still trail in experience/years/management language, partially supporting the scope-inflation hypothesis -- junior roles absorbed new AI requirements but not traditional senior scope. C6 confirms AI terms are SWE-specific, not labor-market-wide (ai z=+65.5 SWE vs control).

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: SWE posting content has shifted substantially between 2024 and 2026, driven primarily by AI/LLM terminology and entry-level role restructuring. Overall unigram JSD (0.0376) matches within-2024 cross-source variation (0.0384), but entry-level JSD (0.0585) is 52% higher -- entry-level postings changed far more than senior ones. AI/ML keyword prevalence surged from 19.2% to 54.9% (broad) and from 3.6% to 24.2% (narrow LLM-specific), with the increase roughly uniform across seniority levels. Entry-level YOE requirements dropped sharply (mean 3.7 to 2.0 years, p < 0.001), suggesting reduced credential inflation rather than increased scope. Among the 74 newly emerging terms (>1% doc prevalence in 2026, <0.1% in 2024), the dominant pattern is AI tooling: agentic, claude, gemini, embeddings, mcp, cursor, codex, langgraph, anthropic.

docs.title: 1. Jensen-Shannon Divergence
docs.text:
JSD measures how different two probability distributions are (0 = identical, 1 = maximally different). Higher values indicate greater vocabulary divergence.

Level n_2024 n_2026 JSD overall 4438 4219 0.0376 entry 745 549 0.0585 mid-senior 2584 3262 0.0387 within-2024 ref (arshkon vs asaniczka mid-senior) 2584 3000 0.0384

The within-2024 reference JSD (0.0384) between arshkon and asaniczka (both early-2024 LinkedIn data) provides a baseline for how much variation exists within the same era due to source differences. The cross-era overall JSD (0.0376) is comparable, suggesting overall SWE vocabulary drift is similar to within-era source variation. However, entry-level JSD (0.0585) is 52% higher than both the overall and reference values, indicating that entry-level postings underwent disproportionately large vocabulary change. This is consistent with AI-driven role restructuring hitting junior roles harder.

docs.title: 2. Emerging and Disappearing Terms
docs.text:
Emerging: >1% document frequency in 2026, <0.1% in 2024. Disappearing: reverse. Document frequency = % of postings containing the term at least once.

Note on artifacts: Many "disappearing" terms are HTML concatenation artifacts from arshkon source formatting (e.g., "skillsa", "positionyou", "chatbotwrite"). These reflect formatting differences between sources, not real vocabulary changes. True substantive disappearances (e.g., "waterfall", "sustainment") reflect real methodology shifts.

docs.title: 3. YOE Inflation (Entry-Level SWE)
docs.text:
Metric 2024 (arshkon) 2026 (scraped) Total entry-level postings 745 549 With YOE extracted 254 (34.1%) 72 (13.1%) Mean YOE 3.7 2.0 Median YOE 3.0 2.0 P25 / P75 2 / 5 1 / 2 % with 0 YOE 0.0% 0.0% % with 3+ YOE 63.4% 19.4% % with 5+ YOE 36.6% 8.3% Mann-Whitney p-value 1.46e-10

Entry-level YOE requirements are lower in 2026 (mean 3.7 -> 2.0).

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
• RQ1 (restructuring): The JSD values and emerging-term analysis confirm that SWE job postings underwent substantial linguistic change from 2024 to 2026. This is not a stable baseline -- the language of hiring has shifted meaningfully.
  • RQ1 (scope inflation): YOE data for entry-level roles provides direct evidence for or against credential inflation at the junior level.
  • RQ2 (task migration): AI term prevalence by seniority shows whether AI requirements are being added uniformly or concentrated at particular seniority levels. Differential adoption rates across levels suggest task migration between seniority tiers.
  • RQ3 (divergence): The broad vs. narrow AI prevalence split helps distinguish genuine technical AI requirements from generic buzzword insertion. The narrow (LLM-specific) measure is more likely to reflect actual tool requirements.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
• JSD computed on arshkon (2024) vs scraped (2026) only. Asaniczka excluded from JSD because it lacks entry-level labels, making seniority-stratified comparisons non-parallel.
  • Description length grew ~50% from 2024 to 2026 (T05 finding). JSD uses normalized frequencies (rates, not counts), providing some but not complete robustness to length differences.
  • The within-2024 reference JSD (arshkon vs asaniczka mid-senior) controls for cross-source variation.
  • YOE extraction is regex-based and may miss non-standard phrasings. Coverage varies by source.
  • AI keyword matching uses regex patterns, which may overcount (e.g., 'AI' in company names) or undercount (novel terminology). The narrow LLM-specific pattern is more conservative.
  • Seniority uses the patched workaround (seniority_patched). Entry-level: arshkon n=745, scraped n=549.

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: Junior/entry SWE roles in 2026 show clear scope inflation in organizational competencies but not in formal credentials. Compared to 2024, entry-level postings in 2026 demand significantly more cross-functional collaboration (+10pp), ownership language (+8pp), end-to-end responsibility (+6pp), and collaboration keywords (+24pp). AI keyword prevalence nearly tripled (15% to 44%). However, median stated YOE actually decreased from 3.0 to 1.0 years, and MS/PhD requirements held flat (~13%). The scope inflation pattern is not junior-specific -- mid-senior roles show similar or larger increases in soft/organizational language, suggesting a field-wide shift in how SWE roles are described, not a junior-targeting squeeze.

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
RQ1 (scope inflation): Strong evidence of scope inflation in behavioral/organizational requirements but not in formal credential requirements (YOE, education). The inflation appears across seniority levels, which complicates the "junior squeeze" narrative. The more precise claim is: employers are demanding more organizational maturity (ownership, cross-functional, end-to-end) at all levels, and entry-level roles are not exempt. The YOE decrease at entry level may reflect a relabeling effect -- formerly "mid-senior" roles now being posted as "entry" with higher soft expectations.

RQ2 (task migration): The ownership/end-to-end/cross-functional increases at junior level (+6-10pp) are consistent with tasks migrating downward: junior engineers are now expected to own more scope. Meanwhile leadership language actually decreased slightly at junior (-4.5pp), suggesting the "lead a team" responsibility is not migrating down.

RQ3 (AI divergence): AI keyword prevalence jumped from 15% to 44% at entry level, with similar jumps at mid-senior (17% to 56%). This establishes the employer-side demand trajectory that RQ3 will compare against actual developer usage.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
1. Extraction coverage: Requirements sections found in 61% of postings; YOE extractable from 51%; education from 54%. Coverage is adequate for aggregate statistics but individual-row extraction has gaps.
  2. YOE paradox at entry level: The median YOE drop from 3.0 (arshkon 2024) to 1.0 (scraped 2026) for entry-level roles is surprising. It may reflect (a) the seniority_patched workaround pulling in genuinely lower-YOE postings that were previously "unknown", or (b) real changes in how entry-level is defined. The arshkon entry-level median of 3.0 YOE is itself suspicious for "entry" roles and may reflect misclassification or ghost posting behavior.
  3. Description length confound: Descriptions grew ~50% from 2024 to 2026 (T05). Raw keyword counts are inflated; length-normalized tech density shows near-zero change (1.2 to 1.3 per 1K chars), while raw tech count rose (4 to 5). Soft skill percentages (binary: mentioned or not) are less affected by length but not immune.
  4. Sensitivity check: Repeating the junior comparison with original seniority_final (smaller n: 213 vs 132) shows consistent direction for most metrics (collaboration +25pp, AI +22pp, ownership +5pp) but weaker magnitudes, supporting the patched results.
  5. Collaboration inflation caveat: The +24pp jump in "collaboration" keywords may partly reflect longer descriptions having more space for boilerplate language about teamwork. Length-normalization for binary flags is not straightforward.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. For T17/synthesis: The scope inflation finding is robust for organizational/behavioral language but must be presented alongside the field-wide baseline shift. Entry-level inflation exists but is not disproportionate relative to mid-senior.
  2. For analysis phase: Use length-normalized tech density (not raw tech count) as the comparable metric. For soft skills, consider a composite "organizational complexity" score (cross-functional + ownership + end-to-end + stakeholder) to reduce multiple-comparison issues.
  3. YOE investigation needed: The entry-level YOE drop warrants deeper investigation -- check whether it is driven by specific companies or a broad shift.
  4. AI keyword list refinement: The current AI regex is broad (includes "ml", "nlp", "machine learning"). For the RQ3 analysis, separate "AI-as-tool" keywords (copilot, ai coding, prompt engineering) from "AI-as-domain" keywords (machine learning, deep learning, NLP).

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: Of the 2,105 unique arshkon SWE companies and 1,563 scraped SWE companies, 379 appear in both periods (18% of arshkon, 24% of scraped by company count; 36% and 40% by posting volume). The within-company vs between-company decomposition reveals that the entry-level share decline is driven by within-company changes (within-company delta: -9.3pp, partially offset by +5.6pp composition effect), while the AI keyword surge is almost entirely within-company (within: +33.0pp vs composition: +1.5pp). Description length growth is also primarily within-company (1.43x for overlap companies vs 1.64x overall), with new-to-2026 companies contributing shorter descriptions on average.

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
RQ1 (restructuring): The entry-level share dropped from 20.6% to 11.3% within the same 379 companies -- a -9.3pp within-company decline. This is stronger than the overall -3.8pp decline because new 2026 companies (scraped-only) have a somewhat higher entry share (14.2%) than the 2026 overlap cohort (11.3%), partially masking the within-company shift. This is robust evidence that the entry-level reduction is not an artifact of sampling different companies; it is happening within continuing employers.

RQ2 (AI adoption): AI keyword prevalence jumped from 18.2% to 51.2% within overlapping companies (+33.0pp). The composition effect is near zero (+1.5pp), meaning new companies entering the 2026 sample do not substantially differ from continuing ones in AI adoption. This supports interpreting the AI keyword surge as a genuine field-wide shift, not a compositional artifact.

Size-band pattern: Large companies (10K+ employees) show the steepest entry-level decline (21% to 8%, -13pp) and the largest AI keyword jump (22% to 58%, +36pp). Small companies (<100) show less entry-level change but equally dramatic AI adoption (9% to 57%). This suggests large-firm restructuring is the primary driver of the entry-level squeeze.

RQ3 context: Firms like Deloitte, Oracle, and PwC went from 0% to 100% AI keyword rates. These are not AI-native companies -- they represent traditional enterprises adding AI language to every SWE posting. This may represent employer signaling rather than genuine task transformation, which is exactly the divergence RQ3 investigates.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
1. Overlap rate: 379 companies overlap out of 2,105 (arshkon) and 1,563 (scraped). The 18-24% overlap rate is expected given the two-year gap and different sampling frames (arshkon = Kaggle snapshot, scraped = active daily scrape). The overlap set is biased toward larger/more prominent companies.
  2. Aggregator contamination: Aggregators like Apex Systems, TEKsystems, and Actalent appear in the top-20 overlap list. Their "entry-level" classifications are questionable (TEKsystems: 89% entry in arshkon vs 15% in scraped). Sensitivity analysis should exclude is_aggregator = true rows.
  3. Size-band data limited to arshkon: Only arshkon has company_size from companion data. The size-band analysis assigns sizes based on arshkon data and applies them to the corresponding scraped postings from the same company. Companies that grew or shrank between 2024 and 2026 may be misclassified.
  4. Scraped-only companies include "Jobs via Dice" (130 rows): This is likely an aggregator that escaped detection. The INDEX.md Gate 1 notes already flagged this. "Turing" (53 rows) is a remote staffing platform and should also be considered an aggregator.
  5. DataAnnotation is arshkon's largest arshkon-only company (163 rows) and was correctly flagged as an aggregator. Its absence from scraped is likely due to it not appearing in 2026 LinkedIn search results rather than the company ceasing to exist.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. For analysis: Use the within-company panel (379 overlapping companies) as the primary identification strategy for RQ1. The -9.3pp within-company entry decline is more credible than the overall -3.8pp because it controls for company composition.
  2. Aggregator sensitivity: Re-run the decomposition excluding is_aggregator = true rows. Several top overlap companies (Apex Systems, TEKsystems, Tata Consultancy) are staffing firms with erratic seniority labels.
  3. Company fixed effects: The 71 companies with 3+ postings in both periods provide a micro-panel suitable for company fixed-effects analysis. This is a stronger identification strategy than cross-sectional comparison.
  4. Scraped-only flag "Jobs via Dice": Add to aggregator list in next pipeline run, or exclude in sensitivity analysis.
