docs.title: T14: RQ3 Employer-Requirement vs Worker-Usage Divergence
Finding
docs.text: AI requirements in SWE postings tripled from 18-21% (2024) to 55% (2026), with the steepest growth in AI-tool-specific mentions (LLM/GPT/Copilot: 3% to 26%). By March 2026, employer-side AI requirements have converged with worker-side usage benchmarks (StackOverflow 2025: 51% daily AI use), closing a gap that was approximately 40 percentage points in 2024. The convergence is not symmetric: employers went from dramatically underspecifying AI relative to worker adoption, to roughly matching it.

docs.title: 1. Posting-side AI requirement rates (SWE, LinkedIn)
docs.text:
Period n Any AI mention AI tools (LLM/GPT/Copilot) AI/ML engineering Broad "AI" 2024-01 (asaniczka) 21,626 18.2% 2.8% 14.8% 12.1% 2024-04 (arshkon) 4,438 20.6% 3.7% 12.4% 15.2% 2026-03 (scraped) 4,219 54.8% 26.0% 29.2% 50.4%

AI-tool mentions (coding assistants, LLMs, generative AI) grew 7-9x. AI/ML engineering mentions (traditional ML, deep learning, NLP, CV) roughly doubled. The broad "AI" catch-all quadrupled. This pattern is consistent with employers beginning to distinguish between "building AI systems" (ML engineering, stable) and "using AI tools for software development" (new, fast-growing).

docs.title: 2. Seniority stratification (seniority_3level_patched)
docs.text:
Period Junior Mid Senior All 2024-01 20.7% (n=121) 19.7% (n=1,258) 18.1% (n=20,247) 18.2% 2024-04 14.8% (n=745) 13.0% (n=207) 18.7% (n=2,601) 20.6% 2026-03 44.1% (n=549) 57.1% (n=112) 55.6% (n=3,287) 54.8%

In 2024, AI requirement rates were roughly uniform across seniority levels (13-21%). By 2026, all levels show massive increases, but junior postings (44%) lag slightly behind mid (57%) and senior (56%). This is notable: employers may be writing AI requirements more aggressively for experienced roles, or junior-targeted postings may be less likely to mention specific technology stacks.

AI-tool-specific mentions by seniority in 2026: junior 22.8%, mid 10.7%, senior 26.2%. The mid-level AI-tool gap is interesting but sample size is small (n=112).

docs.title: 3. Worker-side benchmarks
docs.text: Source Date Metric Rate StackOverflow Developer Survey 2024 Active AI tool use 62% StackOverflow Developer Survey 2025 Daily AI use (professional devs) 51% StackOverflow Developer Survey 2025 Daily AI use (early career, 1-5yr) 55.5% GitHub / industry estimates 2024-25 Regular Copilot use ~26-40% Anthropic Economic Index Jan 2026 Computer & Math share of Claude.ai conversations 34% Gartner forecast 2028 Enterprise AI coding assistant adoption 90% (projected)

docs.title: 4. Divergence analysis
docs.text:
2024 state (large gap, worker > employer): Employers required AI in 18-21% of SWE postings, while 62% of developers reported actively using AI tools (SO 2024). The gap was ~40pp. For AI-tool-specific mentions (Copilot, LLM, GPT), the gap was even larger: 3% employer vs ~33% worker regular use.

2026 state (convergence): Employer AI requirement rate (55%) now approximately matches worker daily use rate (51%, SO 2025). For AI-tool-specific requirements (26%), the gap with estimated Copilot regular use (~33%) has narrowed to ~7pp.

Interpretation: The 2024 divergence reflects a lag where AI tool adoption among developers outpaced employer formalization in job postings. By 2026, employers have rapidly updated posting language to reflect AI as an expected competency. This convergence is consistent with a shift from "AI is a bonus" to "AI is assumed" in SWE hiring expectations.

Caution on causal claims: The convergence could also reflect a composition shift (companies now posting AI-heavy roles more frequently) rather than the same companies updating their requirements. We cannot distinguish these with posting data alone.

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
• RQ3 is supported: there was a meaningful employer-requirement vs worker-usage divergence in 2024 (~40pp gap), which has largely closed by 2026.
  • The closing of the gap suggests rapid institutionalization of AI requirements in SWE hiring, consistent with RQ1's restructuring narrative.
  • The AI-tool-specific category (LLM/GPT/Copilot) is the most analytically useful for RQ3 because it distinguishes "use AI to code" from "build AI/ML systems" (which is a different occupation function). The 3% to 26% trajectory is the cleanest signal.
  • Junior postings showing slightly lower AI requirements than senior is unexpected and worth probing in RQ1/RQ2 analysis. It may indicate entry-level roles are being designed with less technology-stack specificity.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
• AI keyword matching on description (not description_core) to avoid boilerplate removal artifacts. The regex catches common terms but may miss novel phrasing or over-count mentions in boilerplate sections (e.g., "we are an AI company" in company descriptions).
  • The "broad AI" category (\bai\b) likely has some false positives from acronyms in non-AI contexts, but the pattern of 4x growth is robust to reasonable FP rates.
  • Worker-side benchmarks are survey-based (self-reported) and measure different constructs: SO measures "do you use AI tools" while our posting data measures "does the job description mention AI." These are not directly commensurable, but the divergence pattern (large gap in 2024, convergence in 2026) is robust to reasonable measurement differences.
  • Small samples for junior (n=121 to 549) and especially mid (n=112 to 1,258) limit precision of seniority-stratified claims.
  • The 2024-01 period (asaniczka) has 0% unknown seniority (all native-backfilled as mid-senior/associate), which inflates apparent coverage.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
• [ ] For RQ3 analysis: use the AI-tool-specific category (LLM/GPT/Copilot mentions) as the primary employer-side variable, with "any AI" as sensitivity check.
  • [ ] For interview protocol (RQ4): the 3% to 26% AI-tool requirement trajectory is a strong discussion prompt. Ask interviewees whether their teams experienced this shift.
  • [ ] Consider normalizing AI mention rates by description length (T05 found +50% length growth), though the binary indicator (any mention) is less affected by length than count-based measures.
  • [ ] The divergence chart (../figures/T14/t14_divergence_chart.png) is ready as an interview artifact per task spec.
  • [ ] Cross-occupation comparison (T16) should apply the same AI keyword regex to SWE-adjacent and control groups to test whether the 3x growth is SWE-specific.

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: The rule-based ghost_job_risk flag is extremely conservative: only 354 of 1.16M LinkedIn postings (0.03%) score above "low," all of which are entry-level by construction. YOE-seniority contradictions affect 912 total rows (22 SWE), concentrated in entry-level postings requiring 3+ years experience. Description quality is excellent across all sources (>99.8% flagged "ok"), with length outliers (>15K or <100 chars) totaling <0.4% of any source. None of these anomalies threaten analytical validity, but ghost_job_risk will need the LLM-based ghost_assessment_llm to become analytically useful.

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
• Ghost job risk: The current rule-based flag (ghost_job_risk) is too narrow to serve as an analytical variable for RQ1/RQ3. It keys entirely off entry-level seniority_final + high YOE, so it is a special case of YOE-seniority contradiction rather than a broad ghost-job detector. The LLM-based ghost_assessment_llm (Stage 11) will be needed for meaningful ghost-job analysis.
  • YOE contradictions: The 22 SWE contradictions (entry-level postings requiring 3-18 years experience) are genuine scope-inflation signals relevant to RQ1 but too few to support quantitative claims. They serve as qualitative examples.
  • Description quality: Not a concern. Filtering to description_quality_flag = 'ok' loses <0.2% of rows from any source.

docs.title: 1. Ghost job risk profiling
docs.text:
ghost_job_risk n Condition low 1,159,139 All non-entry or entry with YOE < 3 medium 227 entry-level + YOE 3-4 high 127 entry-level + YOE >= 5

All 354 non-low rows have seniority_final = 'entry' by design (the flag only fires for entry-level postings). This is a definitional constraint, not an empirical finding.

By source: arshkon has the most non-low rows (161, 44% of flagged rows, from only 10% of total data), suggesting arshkon entry-level postings disproportionately carry high YOE requirements. Asaniczka has 172 (48%), scraped has 21 (6%).

By period: 2024-01: 172 (49%), 2024-04: 161 (45%), 2026-03: 21 (6%). The lower count in 2026-03 partly reflects fewer total entry-level rows in scraped data and partly the smaller dataset.

Top companies among flagged rows: Medical Diagnostic Laboratories (26), Genesis Biotechnology Group (25), BCG Attorney Search (15), ClearanceJobs (10), Actalent (7). These are primarily staffing agencies and niche employers, not major tech companies. 15% of flagged rows are from aggregators.

SWE specifically: Only 20 SWE postings are flagged (6 high, 14 medium), out of 30,283 SWE rows (0.07%). Ghost job risk is not a meaningful SWE-specific phenomenon in this dataset at current detection sensitivity.

docs.title: 2. YOE-seniority contradictions
docs.text:
912 total rows have yoe_seniority_contradiction = true (0.08% of all rows). 22 are SWE.

SWE seniority_patched total SWE contradiction_n contradiction_pct entry 1,415 19 1.3% associate 1,577 1 0.1% mid-senior 26,007 2 0.0%

The 19 entry-level SWE contradictions include genuine scope inflation examples: "Junior Software Developer" requiring 6 years experience (Amentum), "Entry Level Cloud Engineer" requiring 4 years (Dantech Corporation), and "Software Engineer 1 / Early Career" requiring 18 years (Nutanix -- likely a parsing error in the YOE extractor). Some are title-grade mismatches where the posting title says "Junior" but the description specifies experienced requirements.

Contradiction rate is stable across periods: 0.1% for each source, suggesting no temporal trend in overt scope inflation detectable by the YOE heuristic.

docs.title: 3. Description quality flags
docs.text:
Source Total ok too_short empty ok % kaggle_arshkon 118,165 117,966 199 0 99.8% kaggle_asaniczka 1,014,752 1,014,554 198 0 100.0% scraped 26,576 26,575 1 0 100.0%

Description quality is uniformly high. The too_short flag fires on description_core (post-boilerplate-removal), so some of the 398 too_short rows may have adequate raw descriptions that were over-stripped by the 44%-accuracy boilerplate remover.

docs.title: 4. Description length outliers
docs.text:
Source >15K chars <100 chars Total >15K % <100 % Median length arshkon 111 112 118,165 0.09% 0.09% 3,443 asaniczka 3,103 91 1,014,752 0.31% 0.01% 3,470 scraped 79 1 26,576 0.30% 0.00% 5,106

Very long (>15K): 3,293 total. These are predominantly government/public-sector postings (County of Los Angeles, IRS, Hawaii DOE) with extensive compliance and benefits boilerplate. Zero are SWE except 11 (0.03% of SWE). Not a data quality concern; the boilerplate remover and description_core_llm are designed to handle these.

Very short (<100): 204 total (5 SWE). Includes genuine junk ("CFAO", "On site", "TEST ROLE", "Thank You") concentrated in arshkon (112 of 204). These are effectively empty postings that somehow passed through the source. They are excluded by description_quality_flag != 'ok' if analysis needs clean text.

Description length trend: Scraped data (2026) has median length 5,106 vs 3,443-3,470 for 2024 sources, consistent with the T05 finding of +50% description length growth. This affects keyword counting but not binary AI-mention flags.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
• ghost_job_risk is definitionally limited to entry-level postings; it cannot detect ghost jobs at other seniority levels by design.
  • YOE extraction is regex-based and subject to parsing errors (e.g., the Nutanix 18-year entry-level case is likely a false positive).
  • The lack of temporal trend in contradiction rates (0.1% all periods) does not mean scope inflation is absent -- it means the YOE-heuristic approach lacks power to detect it. Subtler forms of scope inflation (requiring 10 technologies at entry level, or listing "5+ years" in a mid-senior posting) would not trigger the current flag.
  • Description quality flags key off description_core length, not description length, so the 44% boilerplate accuracy issue may inflate the too_short count slightly.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
• [ ] Do not use ghost_job_risk as a primary analytical variable. Wait for ghost_assessment_llm (Stage 11) to enable meaningful ghost-job analysis.
  • [ ] The 22 SWE YOE contradictions can be used as qualitative examples of scope inflation in the RQ1 narrative but should not be cited as quantitative evidence.
  • [ ] For robustness, consider filtering out description_length < 100 rows (204 total, 5 SWE) in text-analysis tasks. This has negligible impact on sample size.
  • [ ] The very long postings (>15K chars) are concentrated in government/public sector. For keyword-rate analyses, consider whether to cap description length or normalize. Binary indicators (any mention) are unaffected.

docs.title: T16: Cross-Occupation Comparison (SWE vs SWE-Adjacent vs Control)
Finding
docs.text: The junior-share decline is SWE-specific, not a macro-labor-market trend. Between April 2024 (arshkon) and March 2026 (scraped), SWE junior share fell from 21.0% to 13.9% (-7.1pp), while control (non-technical) junior share rose from 22.5% to 43.5% (+21.0pp) and SWE-adjacent held roughly steady (18.5% to 20.7%). AI keyword growth, while strongest for SWE (3x), is also present in SWE-adjacent (2.7x) and even control (6.5x from a very low base), indicating AI-as-requirement is a broad phenomenon but AI-tool-specific requirements are concentrated in technical roles. The divergent seniority pattern establishes that the SWE junior decline is not confounded by general hiring trends.

docs.title: 1. Junior share trends (arshkon 2024-04 vs scraped 2026-03)
docs.text:
This comparison uses only arshkon and scraped, the two sources where entry-level native labels exist. Asaniczka (2024-01) cannot be used for seniority comparisons because it has zero entry-level labels -- all its postings are native-labeled mid-senior or associate, artificially showing <1% junior share.

Group Apr 2024 junior % (n known) Mar 2026 junior % (n known) Change (pp) Direction SWE 21.0% (n=3,553) 13.9% (n=3,948) -7.1 DECLINED SWE-adjacent 18.5% (n=1,223) 20.7% (n=565) +2.2 Stable Control 22.5% (n=7,117) 43.5% (n=3,917) +21.0 INCREASED

The SWE junior decline is directionally opposite to both the control group and SWE-adjacent group. This is strong evidence that the decline is occupation-specific rather than driven by macro-economic conditions or platform-level changes in how seniority labels are assigned.

The control group's large increase in junior share may reflect a different dynamic: non-technical occupations are seeing more entry-level postings, possibly due to post-pandemic hiring normalization or LinkedIn usage patterns. This is the opposite of what we see in SWE.

docs.title: 2. Seniority distribution by group (full table, including asaniczka)
3. AI keyword prevalence across groups
docs.text:
Group Period Any AI (%) AI tools (%) AI/ML eng (%) n SWE 2024-01 18.2 2.8 14.8 21,626 SWE 2024-04 20.6 3.7 12.4 4,438 SWE 2026-03 54.8 26.0 29.2 4,219 SWE-adjacent 2024-01 10.2 1.5 8.1 6,491 SWE-adjacent 2024-04 14.7 3.6 8.4 1,397 SWE-adjacent 2026-03 39.6 18.7 21.9 598 Control 2024-01 1.1 0.2 0.1 113,052 Control 2024-04 0.8 0.2 0.2 8,849 Control 2026-03 7.1 1.3 0.4 4,086

Key observations: - AI-tool-specific growth multipliers (2024-01 to 2026-03): SWE 9.3x, SWE-adjacent 12.5x, Control 6.5x. All groups show large relative growth, but absolute levels differ enormously: 26% for SWE vs 1.3% for control. - SWE-adjacent tracks SWE but at ~70% of the SWE rate, consistent with SWE-adjacent roles (DevOps, data engineering, QA automation) requiring similar but not identical AI tool proficiency. - Control AI growth is real but tiny in absolute terms: 0.2% to 1.3% for AI tools. This likely reflects general "AI literacy" language entering non-technical job descriptions, not specific tool requirements. - AI/ML engineering mentions grew 2x for SWE (14.8% to 29.2%) but remained near-zero for control (0.1% to 0.4%), confirming this is a technical-occupation phenomenon.

docs.title: 4. Assessment: Are observed changes SWE-specific?
docs.text:
Junior share decline: YES, SWE-specific. The control group shows the opposite trend (increasing junior share), and SWE-adjacent is stable. This rules out the hypothesis that the junior decline is driven by macro-economic conditions, changes in LinkedIn's platform, or shifts in how seniority labels are assigned across the board.

AI requirement growth: Partially SWE-specific. The absolute level of AI requirements is overwhelmingly concentrated in SWE and SWE-adjacent roles (55% and 40% respectively vs 7% for control). However, the relative growth rate is high everywhere, suggesting AI-as-buzzword is permeating all job descriptions, while AI-as-functional-requirement is concentrated in technical roles. The AI-tool-specific category (LLM/GPT/Copilot) is the cleanest discriminator: 26% for SWE vs 1.3% for control.

Combined pattern: SWE is experiencing a unique combination of (a) declining junior share and (b) surging AI requirements. Control occupations show AI buzzword growth but increasing junior hiring. This strongly suggests the SWE patterns are driven by AI-specific restructuring of the occupation, not by general labor market dynamics.

docs.title: 5. Classification coverage
docs.text:
Group Rows % of total SWE 30,283 2.6% SWE-adjacent 8,486 0.7% Control 125,995 10.9% Other (unclassified) 994,737 85.8%

The "Other" category (85.8% of rows) consists of non-SWE, non-adjacent, non-control postings. These are outside the study's scope. Classification overlaps are negligible (2 SWE+control, 6 adjacent+control).

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
• RQ1 junior-share finding passes the cross-occupation test: the decline is SWE-specific, not confounded by macro trends.
  • RQ3 AI divergence finding is primarily a technical-occupation phenomenon (SWE + adjacent), not a general labor market trend.
  • The control group's increasing junior share may itself be an interesting contrast to cite: while AI is reducing entry-level SWE demand, non-technical occupations continue to grow their entry-level pipeline.
  • SWE-adjacent tracking SWE at ~70% intensity suggests a gradient of AI impact from core SWE to adjacent technical roles to non-technical roles.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
• Asaniczka seniority bias: The 2024-01 period (asaniczka) shows <1% junior share for ALL groups because asaniczka has zero entry-level native labels. The seniority_patched CTE falls through to native_backfill for most asaniczka rows, which only returns mid-senior or associate. Junior share comparisons should use arshkon (2024-04) vs scraped (2026-03) only.
  • SWE-adjacent sample in 2026-03 is small: n=598 total, n=565 with known seniority. Results are directionally informative but not precise.
  • Control group composition may differ by period. Control postings in arshkon may not be the same occupation mix as control postings in scraped data. This limits the precision of the control comparison but does not invalidate the direction (opposite trend to SWE).
  • "Other" occupations (86% of data) are not analyzed. The three-way comparison covers ~14% of rows. The unclassified majority could show different patterns.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
• [ ] For RQ1 analysis: cite the cross-occupation comparison as evidence that junior-share decline is SWE-specific. Use the arshkon-vs-scraped comparison (21.0% to 13.9% for SWE, 22.5% to 43.5% for control) as the primary contrast.
  • [ ] For RQ3 analysis: use the AI-tool-specific category (LLM/GPT/Copilot) as the discriminator between technical-occupation AI requirements and general AI buzzword diffusion.
  • [ ] Consider adding a "broad technical" group (SWE + SWE-adjacent combined) for robustness.
  • [ ] Do NOT use asaniczka for seniority-level cross-occupation analysis. Restrict to arshkon + scraped for any seniority comparison.
  • [ ] The divergent pattern (SWE junior down, control junior up) is a strong finding for the paper narrative and should be featured prominently in the results section.

docs.title: Finding
docs.text: Five interview elicitation artifacts were produced for RQ4 data-prompted interviews. Each artifact translates a quantitative finding from the exploration phase into a concrete, discussable object: (1) five real scope-inflated entry-level JDs from March 2026, (2) five same-company posting pairs showing 2024-vs-2026 evolution at Uber, Amazon, Deloitte, CACI, and Oracle, (3) an annotated junior-share decline chart with AI model release timeline, (4) a senior archetype shift chart contrasting management language with AI orchestration language, and (5) the employer-worker AI divergence chart from T14 showing the gap closing from ~40pp to ~0pp. All artifacts use real posting data and seniority_patched labels.

docs.title: Implication for analysis
docs.text:
The artifacts ground RQ4's reflexive thematic analysis interviews in observed empirical patterns rather than abstract hypotheses. Key prompts enabled:

  • Artifact 1 (inflated JDs): Elicits interviewee reactions to real-world scope inflation -- entry-level postings demanding cross-functional collaboration, ownership, and AI proficiency simultaneously. Tests whether practitioners perceive these requirements as genuine or performative.
  • Artifact 2 (paired JDs): Enables within-company temporal comparison during interviews. The Deloitte pair (.NET Developer -> AI Data Engineer) and Amazon pair (SDE I 2024 -> SWE I 2026 with 3x longer descriptions) are particularly striking.
  • Artifact 3 (junior share + AI timeline): Provides temporal context connecting AI model releases to observed posting changes. Useful for probing causality vs. correlation in interviewees' minds.
  • Artifact 4 (senior archetype): Distinguishes two hypotheses about how senior roles are evolving -- "traditional management amplified" vs. "AI orchestration replacing management." The data shows both: management language increased AND AI language surged.
  • Artifact 5 (divergence): Tests whether interviewees experienced the rapid institutionalization of AI requirements, and whether they perceive employer requirements as reflecting genuine task changes or signaling behavior.

docs.title: Data quality note
docs.text:
• Entry-level postings use seniority_patched, which reduces unknown rate but still depends on native platform labels (the Stage 5 backfill bug workaround). Artifact 1 postings are all genuinely labeled entry-level by LinkedIn's seniority field.
  • Paired JDs (Artifact 2) are not exact role replacements -- they are the best available same-company SWE postings from different periods. Title matching is approximate.
  • The junior-share trend chart (Artifact 3) connects only two data points (arshkon Apr 2024 and scraped Mar 2026). The intervening AI model timeline is suggestive but not causal evidence.
  • The senior archetype data (Artifact 4) combines prevalence rates from T12 (organizational language) and T11 (AI terms by seniority). Some rates were computed on slightly different subsamples (e.g., T12 combined 2024 sources for mid-senior).
  • The divergence chart (Artifact 5) uses StackOverflow Survey data as the worker-side benchmark, which measures self-reported behavior and may not be directly commensurable with posting-side keyword presence.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. Artifacts are stored in exploration/artifacts/ with a README describing each.
  2. Interview protocol should present artifacts without leading interpretation and probe for mechanisms.
  3. The paired JDs (Artifact 2) should be refreshed if the pipeline is re-run with additional scraped data, as more overlap companies may emerge.
  4. After seniority_llm runs, verify that the 5 entry-level postings in Artifact 1 are still classified as entry-level.
  5. Consider adding a 6th artifact showing the cross-occupation contrast (T16: SWE junior down while control junior up) if the interview protocol needs a "macro context" prompt.
